But wait, the 'contains' method in Java checks if the substring exists anywhere in the string. So for example, if the original substring is "ab", and the reversed string has "ba" somewhere, then "ab" is not in the reversed string. So the 'contains' method would correctly return false.

So, the approach is to generate all 2-length substrings of the original string and check if any of them exist as a substring in the reversed string.

So, in code:

public boolean isSubstringPresent(String s) {
    String reversedS = new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString();
    for (int i = 0; i < s.length() - 1; i++) {
        String sub = s.substring(i, i + 2);
        if (reversedS.contains(sub)) {
            return true;
        }
    }
    return false;
}

The time complexity: for each of the (n-1) substrings, we do a contains check, which is O(m) where m is the length of the reversed string, which is n. So overall, O(n^2) time.

But n is up to 100, so n^2 is 10,000, which is acceptable.

solution: 
```java
class Solution {
    public boolean isSubstringPresent(String s) {
        String reversedS = new StringBuilder(s).reverse().toString();
        for (int i = 0; i < s.length() - 1; i++) {
            String sub = s.substring(i, i + 2);
            if (reversedS.contains(sub)) {
                return true;
            }
        }
        return false;
    }
}
```
efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(n^2)
space complexity: O(n)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently checks each possible 2-length substring of the original string against the reversed string, leveraging the `contains` method for quick lookups. This approach ensures that we find any matching substring without unnecessary complexity.
